attributes.stem:
A 63-year-old man is scheduled for an elective inguinal hernia repair. He is a reformed heavy smoker with moderate chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) treated with tiotropium and salbutamol. He reports shortness of breath with moderate exertion (MET>4). His functional capacity is stable and he has not had any recent exacerbations. He is deemed medically optimised by his respiratory physician.

On physical examination, his oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. He is noted to have decreased breath sounds bilaterally, with a prolonged expiratory phase.

Chest X-ray reveals hyperinflated lungs.

His recent spirometry shows

  • FEV1/FVC ratio: 0.65 (> 0.7)

  • FEV1: 65%
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate anaesthetic to optimise this patient's perioperative risk?
attributes.explanation:
Patients with COPD are at an increased risk for perioperative respiratory complications. The anaesthetic technique should be chosen to minimise these risks. Early post-op respiratory complications from general anaesthesia and endotracheal intubation which are more common might include coughing and straining - and these are not ideal for a hernia repair. The procedure and the surgical approach will be discuss between the surgeon and the anaesthetist. Given this patient’s chronic respiratory problems, a local approach would be preferred to a laparoscopic approach.

Bronchospasm and atelectasis in the post anaesthetic care unit may result in an O2 requirement requiring higher level of post-op care eg prolonged recovery stay, HDU etc. Prolonged IPPV is possible but unlikely in this type of patient.

Regional anaesthesia such as spinal or epidural is preferred because it allows the patient to avoid general anaesthesia and its associated complications and the potential need for postoperative ventilation. General anaesthesia with endotracheal intubation is not contraindicated, but it may lead to increased postoperative pulmonary complications in patients with COPD. The pulmonary complications of general anaesthesia include postoperative atelectasis, pneumonia, and exacerbation of COPD due to airway manipulation, which could potentially lead to prolonged mechanical ventilation and increased length of hospital stay.

Local anaesthesia may not provide sufficient pain control for a hernia repair.

Procedural sedation with a benzodiazepine might not provide adequate anaesthesia for the procedure and could potentially cause respiratory depression, which patients with COPD are more sensitive to.

Awake fibreoptic intubation is generally reserved for patients with difficult airways, which is not indicated in this patient's history.
learningPoints: Patients with COPD are at an increased risk for perioperative respiratory complications.
Regional anaesthesia (spinal/epidural) is preferred to general anaesthesia in patients with COPD.
references: Fleisher LA, Fleischmann KE, Auerbach AD, et al. 2014 ACC/AHA Guideline on Perioperative Cardiovascular Evaluation and Management of Patients Undergoing Noncardiac Surgery: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Task Force on Practice Guidelines. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2014;64(22):e77-e137.

Smetana GW, Lawrence VA, Cornell JE; American College of Physicians. Preoperative pulmonary risk stratification for noncardiothoracic surgery: systematic review for the American College of Physicians. Ann Intern Med. 2006;144(8):581-595.
